is a type of defensive system, used in [team sports](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport"), which is the alternative to [man-to-man defense](/wiki/Man-to-man_defense "Man-to-man defense"); instead of each player guarding a corresponding player on the other team, each defensive player is given an area (a zone) to cover.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Defensive strategy in sports

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Zone_defense "Special:EditPage/Zone defense") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Zone defense"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Zone+defense%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Zone+defense%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Zone+defense%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Zone+defense%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Zone+defense%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Zone+defense%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(January 2013)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Zone defense** is a type of defensive system, used in [team sports](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport"), which is the alternative to [man-to-man defense](/wiki/Man-to-man_defense "Man-to-man defense"); instead of each player guarding a corresponding player on the other team, each defensive player is given an area (a zone) to cover.

A zone defense can be used in many sports where defensive players guard players on the other team. Zone defenses and zone principles are commonly used in [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football"), [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football"), [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse"), [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") and [Ultimate frisbee](/wiki/Ultimate_frisbee "Ultimate frisbee") among others.

## Basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zone_defense&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Basketball")]

The names given to zone defenses start with the number of players on the front of the zone (farthest from the goal) followed by the numbers of players in the rear zones. For example, in a [2–3 zone](/wiki/2-3_Zone_Defense "2-3 Zone Defense") two defenders cover areas in the top of the zone (near the top of the key) while three defenders cover areas near the baseline.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[Match-up zone](/wiki/Match-up_zone_defense "Match-up zone defense") is a hybrid man-to-man and zone defense in which players apply man-to-man defense to whichever opposing player enters their area. [John Chaney](/wiki/John_Chaney_(basketball,_born_1932) "John Chaney (basketball, born 1932)"), former head coach of [Temple University](/wiki/Temple_University "Temple University"), is the most famous proponent of this defense. [[2]](#cite_note-2) Hybrid defenses also include [box-and-one](/wiki/Box-and-one_defense "Box-and-one defense"), in which four defenders are in a 2–2 zone and one defender guards a specific player on the offense. A variant of this is [triangle-and-two](/wiki/Triangle_and_Two_Defense "Triangle and Two Defense"), in which three defenders are in a 2–1 zone and two defenders guard two specific offensive players.

Zone defenses are common in international, college, and youth competition. In the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"), zone defenses were prohibited until the 2001–2002 season. The introduction of zone defenses faced resistance from players, including [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan"). Jordan is quoted as saying, "If teams were able to play zone defenses, he said, he never would have had the career he did."[[3]](#cite_note-3)
Other great players, such as [Tim Duncan](/wiki/Tim_Duncan "Tim Duncan"), [Kevin Garnett](/wiki/Kevin_Garnett "Kevin Garnett"), and [Tracy McGrady](/wiki/Tracy_McGrady "Tracy McGrady") have also confirmed that zone defenses made scoring more difficult compared to the 1990s NBA.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
The [defensive three-second violation](/wiki/Defensive_three-second_violation "Defensive three-second violation") rule made it a little more difficult for teams to play zone, since such defenses usually position a player in the middle of the key to stop penetration, but teams adapted by teaching bigs to quickly exit and re-enter the paint, and by running schemes that legally reset the three-second timer.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## American football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zone_defense&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: American football")]

Main article: [Zone defense in American football](/wiki/Zone_defense_in_American_football "Zone defense in American football")

A zone defense in [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") is a type of "pass coverage". See [American football defensive strategy](/wiki/American_football_strategy#Defensive_strategy "American football strategy") and [zone blocking](/wiki/Zone_blocking "Zone blocking").

## Australian rules football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zone_defense&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Australian rules football")]

The zone defence tactic, borrowed from basketball, was introduced into Australian football in the late 1980s by [Robert Walls](/wiki/Robert_Walls "Robert Walls") and revolutionized the game. It was used most effectively by [Essendon Football Club](/wiki/Essendon_Football_Club "Essendon Football Club") coach [Kevin Sheedy](/wiki/Kevin_Sheedy_(Australian_footballer) "Kevin Sheedy (Australian footballer)").

The tactic is used from the [fullback](/wiki/Fullback_(Australian_rules_football) "Fullback (Australian rules football)") kick in after a behind is scored. The side in opposition to the player kicking in places their forward players, including their [full-forward](/wiki/Full-forward "Full-forward") and centre half forward, in evenly spaced zones in the back 50-metre arc. This makes it easier for them to block leading players and forces the kick-in to be more precise, in effect increasing the margin for error which can cause a turnover and another shot at goal. As a result, the best ways to break the zone are for the full-back to bomb it long (over 50 meters), often requiring a low percentage torpedo punt, or to play a short chipping game out of defence and then to switch play as opposition players break the zone. The latter has negated the effectiveness of the tactic since the 1990s.

Another kick-in technique is the *[huddle](/wiki/Huddle "Huddle")*, often used before the zone, which involves all of the players from the non-kicking team huddling together and then breaking in different directions. The kicker typically aims in whichever direction the designated target (typically the [ruckman](/wiki/Ruckman_(Australian_rules_football_position) "Ruckman (Australian rules football position)")) runs in.

## Ice hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zone_defense&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ice hockey")]

In [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), players defend zones in the [neutral zone trap](/wiki/Neutral_zone_trap "Neutral zone trap") and [left wing lock](/wiki/Left_wing_lock "Left wing lock").

## Lacrosse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zone_defense&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Lacrosse")]

In [lacrosse](/wiki/Field_lacrosse "Field lacrosse"), a zone defense is not as often as the normal man defense. It has been used effectively at the [D-III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") level by schools such as [Wesleyan University](/wiki/Wesleyan_University "Wesleyan University"). They almost always use a 6-man “backer” zone, where they have three guys up top and three guys down low and they try to stay in their zone and not rotate as much as possible.[[6]](#cite_note-6) When teams are man down, many teams employ a “box and one” zone defense, where the four outside players stay in their designated zone while the fifth player follows the ball while staying on the crease man.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Netball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zone_defense&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Netball")]

[Netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") is a sport similar to basketball with similar strategies and tactics employed, although with seven players per team. Zone defense is one of the main defensive strategies employed by teams, along with one-on-one defense. Common variants include center-court block, box-and-two zone, diamond-and-two zone, box-out zone and split-circle zone.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zone_defense&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Box-and-one defense](/wiki/Box-and-one_defense "Box-and-one defense")
* [Man-to-man defense](/wiki/Man-to-man_defense "Man-to-man defense")
* [Zonal marking](/wiki/Zonal_marking "Zonal marking")
